document: Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Cri-1 (Singapore - Row 22) PROVISIONALLY

sections.heading: This Act is the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious environmental offences
sections.summary: This section defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against the national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste trade, or wildlife trade, conducted in contravention of domestic and international laws. The definition includes specific activities and attempts to commit such offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines environmental offenses that could impact data transfer assessments involving cross-border environmental data.

requirements.text: Definition of foreign serious environmental offence as an offence against national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste trade, or wildlife trade

sections.source_quote: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described):

sections.heading: This Act is the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (cont.)
Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a
sections.title: Confiscation order may be made despite prior order under Part 9
sections.summary: This section states that a confiscation order under section 6 or 7 may be issued against a person for an act even if a prior confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015 has already been made against that person for the same act. The provision ensures that the court can still impose confiscation orders for the same act if the prior order was under a different part of the law.

requirements.text: A confiscation order under section 6 or 7 may be made against a person in relation to any act despite a prior confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015

sections.source_quote: 8. Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a confiscation order under section 6 or 7 (as the case may be) may be made against a person in relation to any act despite the fact that a confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015 has been made against that person in relation to the same act.

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of property value for confiscation orders
sections.summary: This section defines how the court calculates the value of property for confiscation orders, including adjustments for obligations with priority and gifts caught by the Act. It specifies that the value is determined by the market value of the property minus any priority obligations and gifts that are part of the benefits derived from drug dealing or criminal conduct.

requirements.text: The value of property for confiscation orders is calculated as the market value minus priority obligations and gifts caught by the Act

sections.source_quote: 15.—(1) For the purposes of sections 12 and 13, the amount that might be realised at the time a confiscation order is made against the defendant is — (a) the total of the values at that time of all the realisable property held by the defendant; less (b) where there are obligations having priority at that time — the total amounts payable pursuant to those obligations, to together with the total of the values at that time of all gifts caught by this Act.

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
sections.title: Restriction of realisable property by court order
sections.summary: This section allows the High Court to issue restraint orders prohibiting individuals from dealing with realisable property, with specific conditions. The orders apply to property held by the specified person, including property transferred after the order. The court must notify affected persons and may discharge the order once related criminal proceedings conclude. The order does not apply to property subject to a charge under section 20.
sections.dtia_summary: Restriction of realisable property by court order may impact data transfers if property includes financial data or customer assets. The court's authority to seize and manage property affects cross-border data flows.
sections.authority: General Division of the High Court

requirements.text: The General Division of the High Court may make a restraint order to prohibit any person from dealing with any realisable property
A restraint order may apply to all realisable property held by a specified person, whether described in the order or not
A restraint order may apply to all realisable property held by a specified person, being property transferred after the making of the order
A restraint order may be made only on an application by the Public Prosecutor
A restraint order may be made on an application without notice to a Judge in chambers
A restraint order must provide for notice to be given to persons affected by the order
A restraint order may be discharged or varied in relation to any property
A restraint order is discharged when proceedings for the drug dealing offence or serious offence are concluded
Where a restraint order is made, the General Division of the High Court may appoint the Public Trustee or any person as receiver to take possession of realisable property
Where a restraint order is made, an authorised officer may seize property to prevent removal from Singapore
Property seized under subsection (8) must be dealt with in accordance with the General Division of the High Court's directions

sections.source_quote: 19.—(1) The General Division of the High Court may make a restraint order to prohibit any person from dealing with any realisable property, subject to such conditions and exceptions as may be specified in the order.

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
Where a person is, by reason of section 29, to be taken to have been convicted of
sections.title: Conviction of drug dealing or serious offence: Confiscation order conditions
sections.summary: A court cannot issue a confiscation order based on a person's conviction of a drug dealing offence or serious offence unless the court is satisfied that the person has absconded on balance of probabilities and that the evidence, if unrebutted, would warrant conviction for the offence. This applies to individuals who are taken to have been convicted under section 29.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets conditions for confiscation orders in cases involving convicted drug dealers or serious offenders, requiring proof of absconding and evidence that would warrant conviction if unrebutted. Relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border asset recovery.

requirements.text: Court must be satisfied that the person has absconded on balance of probabilities
Court must be satisfied that evidence, if unrebutted, would warrant conviction for the offence

sections.source_quote: Where a person is, by reason of section 29, to be taken to have been convicted of a drug dealing offence or a serious offence (as the case may be), a court is not to make a confiscation order in reliance on the person's conviction of the offence unless the court is satisfied — (a) on the evidence adduced before it that, on the balance of probabilities, the person has absconded; and (b) having regard to all the evidence before the court, that such evidence if unrebutted would warrant his or her conviction for the drug dealing offence or serious offence, as the case may be.

sections.heading: [29A
sections.title: Substitute property confiscation order conditions
sections.summary: A court must be satisfied that the defendant used or intended to use property for the commission of a drug dealing offence or serious offence, and that the property is not available for forfeiture, to issue a substitute property confiscation order. The order requires payment of the instrumentality's assessed value to the government.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for substitute property confiscation orders, requiring proof of property use in criminal activity and specific valuation rules. Critical for DTIAs involving cross-border asset seizures.

requirements.text: Court must be satisfied that defendant used or intended to use property for commission of offence
Instrumentality must not be available for forfeiture as per subsection (2)

sections.source_quote: Where a defendant is convicted, or is by reason of section 29 taken to be convicted, of a drug dealing offence or a serious offence, the court is to, on the application of the Public Prosecutor, make a substitute property confiscation order against the defendant, if the court is satisfied that — (a) the defendant had used or intended to use any property (called in this Part an instrumentality) for the commission of the offence; and (b) the instrumentality is not available for forfeiture as mentioned in subsection (2).

sections.heading: [29A (cont.)
sections.title: Production orders for financial institutions in drug investigations
sections.summary: This section allows the Public Prosecutor to obtain production orders from financial institutions for material related to drug dealing or serious offences. Financial institutions must produce or grant access to specified materials within 7 days unless the court varies the order. Compliance with such orders is protected from legal disclosure penalties and civil liability. The orders are subject to conditions including reasonable suspicion of drug dealing, material value to the investigation, and public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must comply with production orders for material related to drug dealing or serious offences. These orders require material production within 7 days and are subject to conditions including reasonable suspicion of drug dealing, material value to the investigation, and public interest. This impacts cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must produce or grant access to material within 7 days for drug dealing or serious offence investigations
Production orders require reasonable grounds for suspecting drug dealing or criminal conduct
Material must be of substantial value to the investigation
Material must not consist of items subject to legal privilege
Production must serve public interest
Compliance with production orders is not a breach of disclosure restrictions

sections.source_quote: The General Division of the High Court may, if on such an application it is satisfied that the conditions mentioned in subsection (3) are fulfilled, make an order that the financial institution which appears to the General Division of the High Court to be in possession of the material to which the application relates must — (a) produce the material to the Public Prosecutor or the person duly authorised by the Public Prosecutor for the Public Prosecutor or such person to take away; or (b) give the Public Prosecutor or the person duly authorised by the Public Prosecutor access to the material, within a reasonable period, but not less than 7 days, as the order may specify.

sections.heading: [29A (cont.)
sections.title: Retention of financial transaction records by institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must retain financial transaction documents for a minimum of 5 years from the transaction date or account closure. The retention period varies by document type. Institutions must store records in a retrievable manner and face fines for non-compliance. This includes electronic records and copies in formats approved by the Monetary Authority of Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must retain financial transaction records for 5 years, with specific retention periods for different document types. This impacts data storage duration and record-keeping practices for financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must retain financial transaction documents for 5 years from transaction date or account closure
Records must be stored in a retrievable manner
Institutions must retain copies in approved formats including electronic records

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must retain, or retain a copy of, each financial transaction document for the minimum retention period applicable to the document.

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information
sections.title: Information disclosure to prevent evidence misuse in legal proceedings
sections.summary: This section mandates that individuals who provide information under section 45 must not have possessed that information at any time when it is used for sections 50, 51, 53, 54 and 55A. It establishes that informers' identities must remain confidential in legal proceedings to prevent disclosure of their names or addresses to witnesses, and requires courts to conceal or obliterate entries that could identify informers. The section also outlines conditions under which courts may permit disclosure of informer identities if justice requires it.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by requiring confidentiality of informer identities across legal proceedings and establishing conditions for disclosure to foreign authorities, which may impact cross-border data sharing in criminal investigations.

requirements.text: Person or officer/employee/agent must not have possessed information at any time when used for sections 50, 51, 53, 54 and 55A
Informer information must not be admitted in evidence in civil or criminal proceedings
Witnesses must not disclose informer's name or address
Witnesses must not answer questions that would reveal informer's identity
Courts must conceal or obliterate entries that identify informers
Courts may permit disclosure of informer identities if justice requires it

sections.source_quote: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information under subsection (1) of section 45 as soon as practicable after having the knowledge mentioned in that subsection, the person or officer, employee or agent of the person is to be taken, for the purposes of sections 50, 51, 53, 54 and 55A, not to have been in possession of that information at any time.

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Defence for individuals in criminal arrangements involving proceeds of crime
sections.summary: This section outlines specific defences available to individuals who provide information related to criminal arrangements. To qualify for a defence, the person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement involved proceeds from criminal conduct, that the retention or control of property was facilitated by the arrangement, that they intended to disclose relevant knowledge, or that they had reasonable excuse for failing to disclose. Additionally, for employees, the person must have disclosed relevant knowledge to their employer as per established procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which individuals may claim immunity from liability for transferring or facilitating criminal proceeds, relevant for assessing risks in cross-border financial transactions involving criminal activity.

requirements.text: The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to proceeds from criminal conduct
The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement facilitated retention or control of property
The person must prove they intended to disclose relevant knowledge to an authorized officer
The person must prove they have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure
For employees, the person must have disclosed relevant knowledge to their employer per established procedures

sections.source_quote: Singapore Statutes Online: defence to prove — (a) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement related to any person's proceeds derived from criminal conduct; (b) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that, by the arrangement, the retention or control by or on behalf of the relevant person of any property was facilitated or (as the case may be) that, by the arrangement, any property was used as mentioned in subsection (1) or (1A), as the case may be; (c) that — (i) the person intended to disclose to an authorised officer such knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) in relation to the arrangement; and (ii) there is reasonable excuse for the person's failure to make disclosure in accordance with subsection (3)(a); (d) that, in the case of a person who was in employment at the time in question and the person enters or is otherwise concerned in the arrangement in the course of his or her employment, the person disclosed the knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) to the appropriate person in accordance with the procedure established by his or her employer for the making of such disclosures.

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Defence for individuals in criminal arrangements involving proceeds of crime
sections.summary: This section outlines specific defences available to individuals who provide information related to criminal arrangements. To qualify for a defence, the person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement involved proceeds from criminal conduct, that the retention or control of property was facilitated by the arrangement, that they intended to disclose relevant knowledge, or that they had reasonable excuse for failing to disclose. Additionally, for employees, the person must have disclosed relevant knowledge to their employer as per established procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which individuals may claim immunity from liability for transferring or facilitating criminal proceeds, relevant for assessing risks in cross-border financial transactions involving criminal activity.

requirements.text: The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to proceeds from criminal conduct
The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement facilitated retention or control of property
The person must prove they intended to disclose relevant knowledge to an authorized officer
The person must prove they have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure
For employees, the person must have disclosed relevant knowledge to their employer per established procedures

sections.source_quote: Singapore Statutes Online: defence to prove — (a) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement related to any person's proceeds derived from criminal conduct; (b) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that, by the arrangement, the retention or control by or on behalf of the relevant person of any property was facilitated or (as the case may be) that, by the arrangement, any property was used as mentioned in subsection (1) or (1A), as the case may be; (c) that — (i) the person intended to disclose to an authorised officer such knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) in relation to the arrangement; and (ii) there is reasonable excuse for the: the person's failure to make disclosure in accordance with subsection (3)(a); (d) that, in the case of a person who was in employment at the time in question and the person enters or is otherwise concerned in the arrangement in the course of his or her employment, the person disclosed the knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) to the appropriate person in accordance with the procedure established by his or her employer for the making of such disclosures.

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Proving property benefits in drug or criminal offences without specific offence knowledge
sections.summary: This section establishes that prosecutors do not need to prove a person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe specific property constitutes benefits from drug dealing or criminal conduct. Instead, proving the person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe the property generally relates to such benefits suffices. This applies to multiple offences under sections 50-55A of the Act.

requirements.text: Prosecution must prove person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe property generally relates to drug or criminal benefits
Prosecution does not need to prove person knows specific offence benefits

sections.source_quote: it is sufficient for the prosecution to prove that the person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe that the whole or part of the property constitutes, or directly or indirectly represents, the benefits of an offence generally

sections.heading: The object of this Part is to impose measures for the disclosure of information
sections.title: Object of cross-border cash reporting measures
sections.summary: This section states the purpose of Part 6A is to require disclosure of information about physical currency and bearer negotiable instruments moving into or out of Singapore to detect and prosecute drug dealing and serious offences, and to allow confiscation of contraband currency.

requirements.text: Part 6A aims to detect and prosecute drug dealing and serious offences through cash movement reporting
Part 6A allows confiscation of physical currency or bearer negotiable instruments moved in contravention of reporting requirements

sections.source_quote: The object of this Part is to impose measures for the disclosure of information regarding movements of physical currency and bearer negotiable instruments into and out of Singapore for the purpose of detecting, investigating and prosecuting drug dealing offences and serious offences

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 legislative timeline
sections.summary: This section provides the legislative timeline for the Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024, detailing its bill number (20/2024), first reading date (2 July 2024), and second and third readings (6 August 2024). It also notes the commencement date of 14 November 2024. It contains no substantive legal obligations but serves as a procedural record of the Act's legislative process.
sections.source_quote: 66. Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 Bill: 20/2024 First Reading: 2 July 2024 Second and Third Readings: 6 August 2024 Commencement: 14 November 2024

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — (cont.)
Any person who obstructs or hinders any authorised officer, Suspicious
Section 476
sections.title: Counterfeiting devices or marks for document authentication
sections.summary: This section prohibits counterfeiting devices or marks used to authenticate documents or electronic records, excluding those covered under section 467. It applies to individuals who produce or possess counterfeit marked materials. The prohibition aims to prevent fraud and unauthorized document authentication.

requirements.text: Counterfeiting devices or marks used for authenticating documents or electronic records
Possessing counterfeit marked material

sections.source_quote: Section 476: Counterfeiting a device or mark used for authenticating documents or electronic records other than those described in section 467, or possessing counterfeit marked material

sections.heading: Section 24
sections.title: Hazardous substances storage and use
sections.summary: This section prohibits storing, using, or dealing with hazardous substances in ways that threaten health or safety. It has been deleted by regulation S 387/2025 effective 01/07/2025, so it no longer applies. The prohibition was intended to protect public health and safety from hazardous material misuse.

requirements.text: Storage, use or dealing with hazardous substances

sections.source_quote: Section 24: Storage, use or dealing with hazardous substances in a manner that threatens health, etc. [Deleted by S 387/2025 wef 01/07/2025]

sections.heading: Section 27A(5) (as in force
sections.title: Terrorism-related financial activities prohibited
sections.summary: This section prohibits actions that contravene specific anti-terrorism regulations, including providing funds for terrorists, dealing with terrorist property, providing resources for terrorists, or assisting in terrorist activities. It applies to individuals and entities subject to the Monetary Authority of Singapore's Anti-Terrorism Measures Regulations 2002. The prohibitions are designed to prevent terrorist financing and related activities.

requirements.text: Provision or collection of funds for terrorists
Dealing with property of terrorists
Provision of resources and services for the benefit of terrorists
Doing anything that causes, assists or promotes an act contrary to regulations 5, 6 or 7

sections.source_quote: Section 27A(5) (as in force before 28 April 2023) for contravention of any of the following regulations of the Monetary Authority of Singapore (Anti-Terrorism Measures) Regulations 2002 (G.N. No. S 515/2002) (as in force before 23 September 2013)

sections.heading: Section 37M(4) (previously
sections.title: Falsifying records to obtain cash payouts
sections.summary: This section prohibits falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payouts or PIC bonuses. It applies to individuals involved in financial transactions under the Inquiries Act 2007, targeting fraud and financial misconduct related to cash payments.

requirements.text: Falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payout or PIC bonus

sections.source_quote: Section 37M(4) (previously section 37J(4) (Cap. 134, 2014 Revised Edition)): Falsifying records or using contrivances, etc., to obtain, or assist another person to obtain, cash payout or PIC bonus (or both), etc.

sections.heading: Section 6
sections.title: Payments related to trafficked victims
sections.summary: This section targets individuals who receive payments connected to the exploitation of trafficked victims. It applies under the Remote Gambling Act 2014 and aims to address financial transactions linked to human trafficking.

requirements.text: Receiving payments in connection with exploitation of trafficked victims

sections.source_quote: Section 6: Persons who receive payments in connection with exploitation of trafficked victims

sections.heading: Section 26(3)
sections.title: Failure to comply with organised crime orders
sections.summary: This section prohibits failing to comply with an organised crime prevention order or a financial reporting order. It applies to individuals subject to such orders under the law, with penalties for non-compliance.

requirements.text: Failure to comply with an organised crime prevention order
Failure to comply with a financial reporting order

sections.source_quote: Section 26(3): Failure to comply with an organised crime prevention order or a financial reporting order

sections.heading: Section 26(5)
sections.title: False information for organised crime prevention orders
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing false or misleading information to authorities when applying for an organised crime prevention order or financial reporting order. It applies to individuals who submit such information to relevant authorities. The section does not specify who must comply but implies that those seeking these orders must provide accurate information.

requirements.text: Must not provide false or misleading information for an organised crime prevention order
Must not provide false or misleading information for a financial reporting order

sections.source_quote: Providing false or misleading information for an organised crime prevention order or a financial reporting order

sections.heading: Section 9 (previously section
sections.title: Personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions
sections.summary: This section addresses the unlawful supply of personal information that was obtained in violation of specific legal provisions. It applies to individuals or entities that have acquired such information through illegal means. The section does not specify the exact provisions that are contravened but implies that the information must be obtained unlawfully.

requirements.text: May supply personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions

sections.source_quote: Supplying, etc., personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions

sections.heading: Section 407
sections.title: Criminal breach of trust of property for transportation or storage
sections.summary: This section criminalizes the breach of trust when property is entrusted for transportation or storage purposes. It applies to individuals who have a duty to safeguard property but fail to do so. The section does not specify the exact circumstances under which the breach occurs but implies that the trust must be broken in the context of property handling.

requirements.text: Must not breach trust of property entrusted for transportation or storage

sections.source_quote: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for purposes of transportation or storage

sections.heading: Section 409
sections.title: Criminal breach of trust by public servants and financial entities
sections.summary: This section criminalizes the breach of trust by public servants, bankers, merchants, agents, directors, officers, partners, key executives, or fiduciaries. It applies to individuals who have a duty to safeguard assets but fail to do so. The section does not specify the exact circumstances but implies that the breach must occur in the context of entrusted property.

requirements.text: Must not breach trust by public servant, banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary

sections.source_quote: Criminal breach of trust by public servant, or by banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary

sections.heading: Section 416A
sections.title: Illegally obtained personal information
sections.summary: This section defines illegally obtained personal information as information acquired through unlawful means. It applies to any information that was obtained in violation of legal provisions. The section does not specify the exact legal provisions but implies that the information must be obtained unlawfully.

requirements.text: Illegally obtained personal information is defined as information obtained in contravention of certain provisions

sections.source_quote: Illegally obtained personal information

sections.heading: Section 416(2)
sections.title: Forbearing to oppose or consenting to discharge of bankrupt as inducement
sections.summary: This section prohibits receiving money, property, or security as inducement for forbearing to oppose or consenting to the discharge of bankruptcy. It applies to individuals who may be involved in bankruptcy proceedings. The section does not specify the exact circumstances but implies that the inducement must be related to bankruptcy.

requirements.text: Must not obtain or receive any money, property or security as inducement for forbearing to oppose, or consenting to discharge of bankrupt

sections.source_quote: Obtaining or receiving any money, property or security as inducement for forbearing to oppose, or for consenting to, discharge of bankrupt

sections.heading: Section 39B
sections.title: Unlawful SIM card provision using personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the provision of SIM cards registered with a person's personal information without authorization. It targets unauthorized use of personal data for telecommunications services. The offense applies to individuals and entities involved in the provision of such SIM cards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses unauthorized use of personal data for telecommunications services, which could impact data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibition on providing SIM cards registered using a person's personal information without authorization

sections.source_quote: Section 39B: Unlawful provision of SIM card registered using person's personal information

sections.heading: Section 39D
sections.title: SIM card transacting for criminal activity using another's personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the transaction of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for criminal activities. It targets individuals and entities facilitating illegal communications through unauthorized SIM card usage. The offense applies to those involved in the transaction process.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the use of personal data for criminal activities, which may affect data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Prohibition on transacting SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.source_quote: Section 39D: Transacting of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.heading: Section 39E
sections.title: SIM card transacting for consideration using another's personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the transaction of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for consideration. It targets individuals and entities engaging in unauthorized SIM card usage for financial transactions. The offense applies to those involved in the transaction process.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses unauthorized use of personal data for financial transactions, which could impact data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibition on transacting SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for consideration

sections.source_quote: Section 39E: Transacting of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for consideration

sections.heading: Section 39F
sections.title: Possession of SIM card registered using another's personal information for criminal activity
sections.summary: This section prohibits the possession of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for criminal activities. It targets individuals and entities holding unauthorized SIM cards for illegal purposes. The offense applies to those in possession of such SIM cards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the possession of personal data for criminal activities, which may affect data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Prohibition on possessing SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.source_quote: Section 39F: Possession of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.heading: Section 37S(4)
sections.title: Falsifying records for cash payout in contravention of laws
sections.summary: This section prohibits falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain or assist others in obtaining cash payouts in contravention of domestic and international laws. It targets individuals and entities involved in financial transactions that violate legal requirements. The offense applies to those engaged in fraudulent cash transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses falsification of records for cash transactions, which could impact data transfer assessments involving financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibition on falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payouts in contravention of domestic and international laws

sections.source_quote: Section 37S(4): Falsifying records or using contrivances, etc., to obtain, or assist another person to obtain, cash payout, etc.

sections.heading: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious environmental offences
sections.summary: This section defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against the national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste trade, or wildlife trade, conducted in contravention of domestic and international laws. The definition includes specific activities and attempts to commit such offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines environmental offenses that could impact data transfer assessments involving cross-border environmental data.

requirements.text: Definition of foreign serious environmental offence as an offence against national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste trade, or wildlife trade

sections.source_quote: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described):

sections.heading: Any "foreign serious tax offence", being an offence against the national law of a foreign country
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious tax offence
sections.summary: This section defines a 'foreign serious tax offence' as a tax-related offence against a foreign country's national law that involves wilfully evading or assisting in evading tax through specific actions such as omitting information from returns, making false statements, giving false answers, failing to correct incorrect information, maintaining false records, or using fraud. These actions must be done with intent to evade tax.

requirements.text: Omitting from, or understating or overstating in a return any information which should be included
Making any false statement or entry in any return, claim or application
Giving any false answer to any question or request for information
Failing to inform the tax authority of incorrect information in an assessment
Preparing or maintaining false books of account or records
Using fraud, art or contrivance to evade tax

sections.source_quote: Any "foreign serious tax offence", being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described) wilfully with intent to evade, or to assist any other person to evade, any tax of that country: (a) omitting from, or understating or overstating in a return made for the purposes of that tax any information which should be included in the return; (b) making any false statement or entry in any return, claim or application made, or any document or information required to be given, for the purposes of that tax; (c) giving any false answer, whether verbally or in writing, to any question or request for information asked or made for the purposes of that tax; (d) failing to inform the authority responsible for the collection of that tax, in the required manner, of any incorrect information appearing in any assessment made by that authority, when required to do so; (e) preparing or maintaining, or authorising the preparation or maintenance, of any false books of account or other records, or falsifying or authorising the falsification of any books of account or records; (f) making use of any fraud, art or contrivance, or authorising the use of any such fraud, art or contrivance.

sections.heading: Act 9 of 2018—Cybersecurity Act 2018
sections.title: Cybersecurity Act 2018 legislative timeline
sections.summary: This section provides the legislative timeline for the Cybersecurity Act 2018, detailing the dates of its first reading (8 January 2018), second and third readings (5 February 2018), and commencement date (31 August 2018). It does not contain substantive legal obligations but serves as a procedural record of the Act's legislative process.
sections.source_quote: 42. Act 9 of 2018—Cybersecurity Act 2018 Date of First Reading: 8 January 2018 (Bill /2018) Date of Second and Third Readings: 5 February 2018 Date of commencement: 31 August 2018

sections.heading: Act 7 of 2019—Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering
sections.title: Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering) Act 2019 timeline
sections.summary: This section outlines the legislative timeline for the Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing) Act 2019, including its first reading date (14 January 2019), second and third readings (11 February 2019), and commencement date (10 April 2019). It contains no substantive legal obligations but documents the Act's legislative process.
sections.source_quote: 44. Act 7 of 2019—Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing) Act 2019 Date of First Reading: 14 January 2019 (Bill /2019) Date of Second and Third Readings: 11 February 2019 Date of commencement: 10 April 2019

sections.heading: Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024
sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 legislative timeline
sections.summary: This section provides the legislative timeline for the Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024, detailing its bill number (20/2024), first reading date (2 July 2024), and second and third readings (6 August 2024). It also notes the commencement date of 14 November 2024. It contains no substantive legal obligations but serves as a procedural record of the Act's legislative process.
sections.source_quote: 66. Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 Bill: 20/2024 First Reading: 2 July 2024 Second and Third Readings: 6 August 2024 Commencement: 14 November 2024

sections.heading: G.N. No. S 256/2026—Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes
sections.title: Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992 Amendment Order 2026
sections.summary: This section details the amendment to the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992 via Order 2026, which came into effect on 1 May 2026. It includes a comparative table showing renumbering of provisions between the 2000 and 2020 editions of the Act, with some sections deleted by subsequent legislation. The section does not contain substantive legal obligations but provides historical context and amendments.
sections.source_quote: 74. G.N. No. S 256/2026—Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992 (Amendment of Second Schedule) () Order 2026 Date of commencement: 1 May 2026
